content.full_text:
Fern Por-rrrcAr- PnacrrcBs CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento. CA 9-581.1-2-129
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
June 25, 2009
Eric A. Ziedrich
Mayor, City of Healdsburg
P.O. Box 970
Healdsburg, California 9 5448
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-09-128
Dear Mr. Ziedrich:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (The "Act")r and is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71.) Additionally, we
base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if
any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest,
Government Code Section 1090, or Govemment Code Section 1099 regarding the
holding of incompatible offices. Because you seek general information, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.'
QUESTTON
Do you have a potential conflict of interest participating in governmental decisions
as a member of the North Coast Rail Authority when you own property located adjacent to,
and across the street from, the North Coast Rail Authority railroad tracks?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Secrions 8 I 000 through 9 I 0 14. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through'18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
: Intbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).


File No. I-09-128
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
You have a conflict of interest participating in any governmental decision that
would serve to bring about the activation of the railroad line running past your property.
However, after any and all decisions are made to implement this goal, you may be able to
participate in those decisions that can be segmented, as discussed below, from any decision
that would affect the activation of the railroad line within 500 feet of your property.
FACTS
You are the mayor of the City of Healdsburg and a board member on the North
Coast Rail Authority (the "NCRA"), which is a multicounty authority created by
legislation in 1989 (Govemment Code Section 93000 et seq.) with a duty to reactivate the
rail running from Napa County, through Marin, Sonoma, Mendocino, and Humboldt
Counties.3
You also own a business, Healdsburg Lumber Company, located at359 Hudson
Street in the City of Healdsburg. Your business is located adjacent to, and across the
street from, NCRA's railroad tracks running through Healdsburg. You own the business
and the real estate.
Because NCRA operates for almost the sole purpose of reactivating the railroad
line, and your property would likely appreciate in value due to the reactivation of the line,
you seek advice as to what level of participation you may engage in with respect to
general issues that impact the entire railroad operation."
A1\ALYSIS
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
3 According to information provided on the NCRA website (www.northcoastrailroad.org), which
you have included in your request, "[t]he Act was intended to ensure continuation of railroad service on the
Northwestern Pacific (NOW) rail line, and envisioned the railroad playing a major role in the transportation
infrastructure serving the North Coast. In creating the NCRA to restore and preserve rail service, the
Legislature recognized that California's North Coast region suffers from restricted access and limited
transport options.
a Because you have specifically asked for advice regarding your position on the board of the North
Coast Railroad Authority our analysis is limited to that position, and we are not addressing any potential
conflicfof-interest issues that may arise from your position as mayor.


File No. I-09-128
Page No. 3
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic .
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framework. (Regulation 18700(b)
(l)-(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)
Step One: Are You a Public Official?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local govemment agency." (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) You have indicated
that you are a member of the board of the North Coast Railroad Authority ("NCRA").
You have not included any information about whether NCRA is a local government
agency or not, but for purposes of our analysis, we will assume that it is and, that as a
member of that agency, you are a public official.
Step Two: Will You Be Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)
A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or
she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2)s A public official is "influencing a
governmental decision" if he or she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant regarding the decisions.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
As a member of the NCRA, you will be called upon to participate in and make
decisions on the issues facing NCRA relative to the authority vested in it. Therefore, you
will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official positions to
infl uence a governmental decisiorr.
t Making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a public
official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly owned real
property or business entity. (See Regulation 18702.4.)


File No. I-09-128
Page No.4
Step Three: What are Your Economic Interests-The Possible Sources of Conflict
of Interest for Him?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
t8703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income
that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 103(e); Regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
You have indicated that your own certain real property on which your business is
located. We assume your interest in the real property is valued at $2,000 or more. We
also assume your interest in the business, Healdsburg Lumber Company, is also valued as
$2,000 or more. Therefore, you have an economic interest in the business and the real
property because of your investment. Additionally, you have an economic interest in the
business as the owner of that business. Finally, as the owner of the business you have an
economic interest in the business as a source of income to you, and you also have a
source of income economic interest in any customers of the business who provide you
income or $500 or more within 12 months of the governmental decision.


File No. t-09-128
Page No. 5
Step Four: Are Your Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision? 6
Real Property: Real property in which a public official has an economic interest
is considered directly involved if the property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries
of the property that is the subject of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18704.2(a)(l).)
Under the facts you have presented, the "real property [that] is the subject of the
decision" is the railroad line, which runs through several counties and within 500 past
your property. Therefore, your property is considered directly involved in any decision
that affects the entire line, or that portion of the line running past your property.
Business Entity and Source of Income: A business entity or a source of income
is directly involved in a decision in a govemmental decision when that person:
"(l) lnitiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1 (a).)
You have not given any specific facts relating to any particular governmental
decision, but unless the business entity or source of income meets one of the above
criteria, those economic interests are consider'ed indirectly involved in the govemmental
decision for purposes of determining the applicable materiality standard.
Step Five: What Are the Applicable Materiality Standards?
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property that is directly involved in the governmental decision is
presumed to be material.
"The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect
on the real property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l).
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known, appropriately, as the "one penny rule." In order to rebut
the presumption, you would have to establish that the decision would not affect the
6 When an economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, fbr purposes of
determining the materiality standards, it is considered indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).)


File No. I-09-128
Page No. 6
property's value by even one cent. Because you have stated that the reactivation of the
railroad would likely result in increased value to your property, the materiality standard
has, therefore, been met. Accordingly, any governmental decision that affects the
railroad line running within 500 feet of your property would be material.T
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards for indirectly involved
business entities. Because your question is directed to your real property interest, you
have not provided any information regarding the size of your business or the nature of
any potential governmental decision that may have an affect on this business. Therefore,
we are unable to analyze this interest. Please be aware, however, that any governmental
decision that may have a financial effect on your business, whether the decision affects
the rail within 500 feet of your business or not, may give rise to a conflict of interest as a
result.s
Step Six: Reasonable Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) However, it is reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.)
Given the facts you have presented it appears that your property will increase in
value as the result of any governmental decision that will promote the reactivation of the
railroad that include the portion adjacent to your property. Accordingly, unless the
governmental decision will have affect on that portion of the line, you have a conflict of
interest and may not participate in the decision.
With respect to your business, because we have no information with respect to
any potential govemmental decision we are unable to analyze whether or not it would
reasonably foreseeably have a material financial effect on your business.
Segmentation:
We have previously advised, however, that large and complex decisions may,
under certain circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has
a disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other
7 The materiality standards for indirectly involved business interests and source of income are
listed in Regulationsl8T05.l and 18705.3. Because you have not provided any information with respect to
any potential affect ofany governmental decision on these interests, and because we have found a material
financial effect on your property (to which your inquiry is limited), we merely provide this information for
your consideration and will not further analyze these factors. If you find that you may have a particular
governmental decision that could potential atfect these interest, please write again for further issistance.
8 For example, a governmental decision to build a structure proximate to the railroad line, say two
miles away, that would require building materials that would likely be purchased from your business.


File No. I-09-128
Page No. 7
components in which the official has no financial interest. (Regulation 18709, enclosed;
Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the
following conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated
to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in
a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the
official was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision."
In the past, the Commission has advised that some decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (See generally Yang Advice Lefter, No. I-06-
198; Stone Advice Letter, No. 4-06-0O7; Barker Advice Letter, A-05; Hull Advice
Letter, No. 4-04-052.) However, segmentation may apply when decisions are broken
down into separate decisions that are qot inextricably interrelated to other decisions in
which you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.
While you have not provided sufficient information to allow us to analyze this,
given the nature of the NCRA board and the large area over which the railroad is
proposed to be reactivated, there are likely to be decisions that will not necessarily affect
the potion of the line running past your property. Please keep in mind, however, that any
decisions that could be considered implementation decisions, that is, decisions that would
affect the reactivation of the entire line in general, would be problematic, and you would
not be able to participate in those decisions. Those decisions should be considered and
decided, without your participation, before any other decisions that could affect them.


File No. I-09-128
Page No. 8
Because you have not provided any information on particular govemmental
decision, we are only able to provide you with general information in this regard. Once
you have determined the nature of any particular decision, if you need further assistance,
please write with the facts available and we will provide further analysis.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
You have not given any facts indicating that the "public generally" exception may
apply, or to indicate, the "legally required participation" exception would be necessary.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By:
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
wJLjgl
Enclosure

affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision."
In the past, the Commission has advised that some decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (See generally Yang Advice Lefter, No. I-06-
198; Stone Advice Letter, No. 4-06-0O7; Barker Advice Letter, A-05; Hull Advice
Letter, No. 4-04-052.) However, segmentation may apply when decisions are broken
down into separate decisions that are qot inextricably interrelated to other decisions in
which you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.
While you have not provided sufficient information to allow us to analyze this,
given the nature of the NCRA board and the large area over which the railroad is
proposed to be reactivated, there are likely to be decisions that will not necessarily affect
the potion of the line running past your property. Please keep in mind, however, that any
decisions that could be considered implementation decisions, that is, decisions that would
affect the reactivation of the entire line in general, would be problematic, and you would
not be able to participate in those decisions. Those decisions should be considered and
decided, without your participation, before any other decisions that could affect them.
Because you have not provided any information on particular govemmental
decision, we are only able to provide you with general information in this regard. Once
you have determined the nature of any particular decision, if you need further assistance,
please write with the facts available and we will provide further analysis.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
You have not given any facts indicating that the "public generally" exception may
apply, or to indicate, the "legally required participation" exception would be necessary.

QUESTION: Do you have a potential conflict of interest participating in governmental decisions
as a member of the North Coast Rail Authority when you own property located adjacent to,
and across the street from, the North Coast Rail Authority railroad tracks?
: Intbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

CONCLUSION: You have a conflict of interest participating in any governmental decision that
would serve to bring about the activation of the railroad line running past your property.
However, after any and all decisions are made to implement this goal, you may be able to
participate in those decisions that can be segmented, as discussed below, from any decision
that would affect the activation of the railroad line within 500 feet of your property.

Ziedrich Mayor, City of Healdsburg P.O. Box 970 Healdsburg, California 9 5448 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No.I-09-128 Dear Mr. Ziedrich: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (The "Act")r and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest, Government Code Section 1090, or Govemment Code Section 1099 regarding the holding of incompatible offices. Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.' QUESTTON Do you have a potential conflict of interest participating in governmental decisions as a member of the North Coast Rail Authority when you own property located adjacent to, and across the street from, the North Coast Rail Authority railroad tracks? ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Secrions 8 I 000 through 9 I 0 14. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through'18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. : Intbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). File No. I-09-128 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION You have a conflict of interest participating in any governmental decision that would serve to bring about the activation of the railroad line running past your property. However, after any and all decisions are made to implement this goal, you may be able to participate in those decisions that can be segmented, as discussed below, from any decision that would affect the activation of the railroad line within 500 feet of your property. FACTS You are the mayor of the City of Healdsburg and a board member on the North Coast Rail Authority (the "NCRA"), which is a multicounty authority created by legislation in 1989 (Govemment Code Section 93000 et seq.) with a duty to reactivate the rail running from Napa County, through Marin, Sonoma, Mendocino, and Humboldt Counties.3 You also own a business, Healdsburg Lumber Company, located at359 Hudson Street in the City of Healdsburg. Your business is located adjacent to, and across the street from, NCRA's railroad tracks running through Healdsburg. You own the business and the real estate. Because NCRA operates for almost the sole purpose of reactivating the railroad line, and your property would likely appreciate in value
